title: How Your Paycheck Works

protagonist.name: Jordan
protagonist.context: Just started her first full-time job out of high school at $52,000/year

narrative.hook_question: Why did Jordan's first paycheck say $1,538 instead of $2,000?

learning_objectives: Distinguish gross pay from net pay
Identify which paycheck deductions are mandatory vs optional
Explain why federal tax is progressive
Correctly apply marginal tax bracket reasoning
Calculate net pay from a gross figure and a set of deduction rates
Budget from net pay rather than gross salary

standards.note: Replace with verified Jump$tart standard IDs before pilot.

message.body: I just got my first paycheck!! Something's wrong — it says $1,538 but my salary is $52,000. Can you help me figure out what happened?

steps.prompt: Before we dig in — where do you think the missing $462 went?
steps.subtitle: No wrong answer. Just your best guess.

options.label: An error she needs to fix

steps.callback_phrasing: You guessed {label}. Here's how close that was:

steps.heading: Let's unpack Jordan's paystub
steps.instruction: Tap each line to reveal what it is.

items.label: Gross Pay
items.caption: What Jordan's contract says she earns every two weeks — before anything is taken out.

items.label: Federal Income Tax
items.caption: Goes to the IRS. Pays for national programs — military, roads, agencies.

items.label: State Income Tax
items.caption: Jordan lives in Michigan. Nine states don't have this — Texas and Florida, for example.

items.label: Social Security
items.caption: 6.2% of gross. Funds retirement income Jordan will collect when she's older.

items.label: Medicare
items.caption: 1.45% of gross. Funds healthcare coverage at age 65+.

items.label: Net Pay
items.caption: What actually hits Jordan's bank account. (Her real check was $1,538 — she has one more deduction we'll uncover later.)

steps.completion_prompt: So the missing money went to taxes and deductions. Let's make sure that sticks.

steps.prompt: Looking at Jordan's paystub: which number is her gross pay?
options: $2,000 — the total before anything is removed

feedback.correct: Right. Gross is the top line — before deductions.
feedback.incorrect: $1,627 is what hit the bank. Gross is the number *before* anything is taken out — the $2,000 at the top of the stub.

steps.prompt: Which of these come out of every paycheck — and which only if Jordan chooses them?

fed.wrong: Federal tax is withheld by law — no opt-out.

ss.wrong: Social Security (6.2%) is part of FICA — mandatory.

medicare.wrong: Medicare (1.45%) is part of FICA — mandatory.

401k.wrong: 401(k) is a retirement plan Jordan chooses to join.

health.wrong: Health insurance is an elected benefit — she can decline.

union.wrong: Union dues only apply if she's in a union role.

steps.completion_feedback: The mandatory column is basically 'the government.' The optional column is Jordan's choices — most of which are good ideas, but still choices.

steps.prompt: Drag the slider to see what percent of income the federal government takes at different salary levels.
steps.live_readout: At {salary}, the effective federal rate is about {rate}%.
steps.question: What pattern are you seeing as salary goes up?
options: Higher earners pay a higher percent (progressive)
Higher earners pay a lower percent (regressive)

feedback.correct: Right. The U.S. federal system is progressive — the rate climbs with income.
feedback.incorrect: Watch the bar as you drag from $25K to $180K. The rate keeps rising — that's what 'progressive' means.

setup.message: My manager said I can get a $2,000 raise but warned me it might push me into a higher tax bracket and I'd take home less money overall. Should I turn it down??

pre_question.prompt: Quick gut check — is Jordan right to worry?
options: Yes — crossing into a higher bracket means the whole salary gets taxed higher
No — only the dollars above the bracket line get taxed higher; the raise always helps

readout_lines: Before: Jordan owes about ${before_tax} in federal tax.
After: Jordan owes about ${after_tax} in federal tax.
The extra $2,000 is taxed at {marginal_rate}% — not her whole salary.

post_reveal_question.prompt: So what should you tell Jordan?
options: Turn down the raise — she'll actually lose money
Take the raise — she'll keep most of it, and only the new dollars hit the higher rate
Take the raise only if she also increases her 401(k)
It depends on which state she lives in

feedback.correct: Exactly. A raise can never reduce her take-home — that's a myth the bracket system causes people to believe.
feedback.incorrect: Check the readout again: her take-home went UP, not down. A higher bracket only applies to the dollars above the line.

steps.intro: Time to build Jordan's real paystub. Use the rates on the right to fill in each deduction.

fields.hint_on_wrong: Health insurance is a flat $48.10 — not a percentage.

fields.expected_formula: gross - fed - state - ss - medicare - health
fields.hint_on_wrong: Net = Gross minus every deduction. Add them up and subtract from $2,000.

on_complete.message: This is Jordan's actual paystub. The real number — $1,578.90 — matches what she told us at the start, once you include the health insurance she'd signed up for.

steps.message: Okay, unrelated — my friend Maya just got a $45,000 offer in California and signed a lease for $1,600/month. She's pumped. Is she okay?
steps.prompt: Run the math on what $45,000 actually delivers per month. Is $1,600 rent sustainable?
options: She's fine — $45K/12 = $3,750/month, so rent is under half
She's in trouble — her take-home is closer to $2,700/month, so $1,600 eats almost 60% of it
She's fine as long as she doesn't have a car payment
Impossible to say without knowing her credit score

feedback.correct: Right. $45K gross ≈ $2,700 net per month. $1,600 rent on that is a tight, risky position — and she committed before checking.
feedback.incorrect: She budgeted from gross ($3,750/month). But take-home on $45K is closer to $2,700. That $1,050/month gap is the exact mistake this lesson is about.

steps.callback_phrasing: At the start, you guessed the missing money went to {predicted_label}. {accuracy_line}
steps.prompt: In one sentence, how would you explain the gap to Jordan?
phrase_bank: Your salary is what you earn; your paycheck is what's left after mandatory deductions.
The government takes taxes and FICA before the money ever reaches your account.
Budget from the number that shows up in your bank — not the number on your offer letter.
About 20–25% of your gross pay goes to taxes and Social Security every paycheck.
steps.completion_message: Nicely done. Next time someone gets a raise or a job offer, you'll know which number actually matters.

final_evaluation.task_description: The build_artifact step (s8) doubles as the performance task. A correctly completed paystub with all 6 fields within tolerance = lesson passed. Teacher dashboard shows per-field correctness.
final_evaluation.rationale: Classic end-of-lesson quizzes duplicate what the performance task already measures. Building a correct paystub is the evidence of learning.

discussion_prompts: Jordan budgeted $1,500/month in rent based on her $52,000 salary. What should she do now?
Nine states have no state income tax. Is that good or bad for residents? What's the tradeoff?
FICA is a flat 7.65% for everyone regardless of income (up to a cap). Is that fair? Why or why not?
teacher_layer.exit_ticket: Write one sentence explaining the difference between gross and net pay to a younger sibling.

requirements: All drag-drop interactions have keyboard equivalents (Tab to select, Space to pick up, Arrow to move, Enter to drop).
Every color-coded state (correct/incorrect, mandatory/optional) also uses an icon or label.
Slider exposes aria-valuenow, aria-valuemin, aria-valuemax and a live-region readout.
Minimum contrast 4.5:1 for body text, 3:1 for large text.
All interactive elements reachable by keyboard in logical order.
